text:
A lot of people are saying “invest in photonics, quantum, deep tech”

Reality is most retail including me don’t actually have an edge there

Unless you’re building this stuff or working in it every day, you’re mostly guessing

Like I can look at $COHR $LITE $POET and read about them

But do I really know what separates one from the other at a deep level?

And more importantly would I know when one quietly falls behind? Probably not

Same thing with chips

I see $NVDA $AMD $ASML $TSM all winning from the data center buildout
But if something shifts and one starts losing would I catch it early? Unlikely

That’s why I own $SMH than pretend I can out analyze engineers

Your edge isn’t chasing the most technical thing

Your edge is understanding what you actually use and see every day

I use $META daily. Everyone around me uses it. I can feel if engagement is going up or down

That’s real signal

There’s always going to be a “next bottleneck” or the next shiny theme

Most people chasing it are just rotating narratives not actually investing with an edge

Stay within what you understand my friends

text:
@bdinvestingg The “photonics kills copper” take misses the point.
Photonics moves data. Copper delivers power.
With ~200GW of data centres in backlog, that’s 5–8M tonnes of copper demand waiting.
AI isn’t reducing copper—it’s increasing it.

$MXL Recap (2/2)

ℹ️ Setup: Gap & Go 

Undiscovered name that's supplying the photonics boom, 140% this week!

➡️Saw constant buying pressure in premarket
➡️Joined in the trend early > scaled into the 50 psych breakout 
➡️Held into open  as we got a push towards 60 (no 60 fill on am move🫠)
➡️Clouds held and great higher low price action into 10am so I scaled in more as PA got tight
➡️Got the move to HOD > 60 breakout!
➡️Locked into strength over 60
➡️wanted 65 on this but gave up midday
➡️Stopped out when 5/12 clouds broke

text:
Copper is dead. AI data centers need light — and photonics companies can’t build fast enough to keep up.

$LITE – Makes the lasers inside every optical transceiver. No LITE, no light.
$AAOI – Builds transceivers that convert electrical signals to optical. The translator.
$MRVL – Designs the silicon brains that control photonic data flow at speed.
$GLW – Invented optical fiber. Still makes the glass the internet runs through.

text:
Three distinct approaches to quantum hardware currently dominate the sector's market activity: cloud-accessed trapped ions, quantum annealing with hybrid solvers, and room-temperature integrated photonics.

The industry is not converging on a single architecture. It is branching out, with different players betting on fundamentally unique paths to commercial value. This diversity proves the field is in its experimental prime, where the ultimate hardware winner remains undecided and may never narrow to just one modality.

Yet the true common thread is accessibility. Through cloud delivery, advanced developer frameworks, and intuitive circuit creation platforms, the race has permanently evolved. It is no longer just a raw contest of qubit counts. The defining metric is how quickly platforms can put usable quantum capability into the hands of enterprise teams.

The commercial layer of quantum computing is forming in real time. The organizations building robust bridges between complex physics and practical business problems are the ones shaping what comes next #QuantumComputing

text:
什么是硅光子（Silicon Photonics）
最简单的理解：
传统芯片用电子传输信息，硅光子用**光子（光）**传输信息。

传统方式：电信号 → 铜线传输 → 慢、热、耗能
硅光子：光信号 → 光纤传输 → 快、冷、省电

硅光子在AI里的具体应用
Co-Packaged Optics（CPO，共封装光学）：
这是最重要的趋势，把光模块直接封装到交换机芯片旁边：

传统方式：
交换机芯片 → 电信号 → 插拔式光模块 → 光纤

CPO方式：
交换机芯片 + 硅光子模块（合为一体）→ 光纤

text:
I watched sunlight flicker on water.

The light appeared. Disappeared. Appeared again.

I thought: what if that IS a logic gate?

That question became 28 verified claims,
a complete compiler, and a new computing paradigm.

🧵 The Photonic Logic Chip. Thread below. 👇

#photonics

text:
$POET v $MRVL

David Lazovsky is a mechanical engineer who has over 50 patents in semiconductors and photonics. He became Executive Chairman of $POET in 2017. Before his arrival, $POET focused on GaAs. It was Lazovsky who pushed $POET to develop their optical interposer. He left $POET in 2020 and created Celestial AI because he was more keen on developing the whole photonics ecosystem. The essence is how to move data around multiple logical chips and memory, optically. Optical Multi-Die Interconnect Bridge, US Patent #12399333 and Photonic Memory Fabrics, US Patent #12271595. His development at Celestial AI was based on the optical interposer from $POET. Celestial AI signed a purchase agreement with $POET. 

$MRVL acquired Celestial AI in Feb 2026, and Lazovsky became a senior member of $MRVL. This is when the problem started, I think. $MRVL didn't spend $3.25 billion on outsourcing one of the key components in their future business. They wanted a vertically integrated system.

Lazovsky must know the gory details of $POET's optical interposer. And yet, it belongs to $POET. It looks like $MRVL decided to develop their own interposer instead of outsourcing it from $POET. The incident, breach of NDA, was just an excuse, I believe. Because the partnership between $POET and Celestial AI was known for years.

$POET lost an exceptional business opportunity. They need to find another. $MRVL and Lazovsky need to come up with a different solution to make his system work without $POET's interposer. He is a smart guy and he may have an idea already. But will it be different enough for him to be able to avoid an IP battle? $POET looks screwed, $MRVL is getting into a risky business development. But somehow I don't find this will kill $POET, because their optical interposer can work in a different setting and doesn't depend on Lazovsky's fabric. It's the other way around at the moment.

He became Executive Chairman of $POET in 2017. Before his arrival, $POET focused on GaAs. It was Lazovsky who pushed $POET to develop their optical interposer. He left $POET in 2020 and created Celestial AI because he was more keen on developing the whole photonics ecosystem. The essence is how to move data around multiple logical chips and memory, optically. Optical Multi-Die Interconnect Bridge, US Patent #12399333 and Photonic Memory Fabrics, US Patent #12271595. His development at Celestial AI was based on the optical interposer from $POET. Celestial AI signed a purchase agreement with $POET. $MRVL acquired Celestial AI in Feb 2026, and Lazovsky became a senior member of $MRVL. This is when the problem started, I think. $MRVL didn't spend $3.25 billion on outsourcing one of the key components in their future business. They wanted a vertically integrated system. Lazovsky must know the gory details of $POET's optical interposer. And yet, it belongs to $POET. It looks like $MRVL decided to develop their own interposer instead of outsourcing it from $POET. The incident, breach of NDA, was just an excuse, I believe. Because the partnership between $POET and Celestial AI was known for years. $POET lost an exceptional business opportunity. They need to find another. $MRVL and Lazovsky need to come up with a different solution to make his system work without $POET's interposer. He is a smart guy and he may have an idea already. But will it be different enough for him to be able to avoid an IP battle? $POET looks screwed, $MRVL is getting into a risky business development. But somehow I don't find this will kill $POET, because their optical interposer can work in a different setting and doesn't depend on Lazovsky's fabric. It's the other way around at the moment."}}}, "rest_id": "2048901067467862263", "source": "Twitter for iPad", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "126", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

as promised, here is more on photonics 

1 with buy signals, 7 with sell signals

Sector running hot. Signals have to scream Sell due to overextended. But a 'Sell' signal doesn't always mean a crash; sometimes it just means a rest stop. It’s possible the engines are just cooling down before the next leg up. The trend isn't broken … til proven otherwise

$MRVL , $AAOI , $AEHR , $LPTH, $LWLG , $UMC , $NOK , 
$SIVEF

I’ll give you two. The MORAN AI Bottleneck Index says: 👇 

The booming AI buildout has 2 large bottlenecks which lie in Photonics/Optics & Memory. 

*Roundhill Memory ETF (DRAM)*
- Holds giant memory names: 
Samsung, SK-Hynix, Micron, Sandisk. 

*Fabrinet (FN)*
- Largest Optics packaging for AI boom.

If you want the full MORAN terminal, DM me. It’s better than Bloomberg’s terminal & they don’t measure AI’s Bottlenecks like this. 

The next major bottleneck is Power (problems already emerging). We will need to take energy intensive compute to orbit to alleviate that. 

Counting on SpaceX for bringing Starship (fully reusable rocket w/ 4x more payload than Falcon 9) online with higher launch cadence. 🚀

text:
Which earnings are you looking forward to most this week?

We’re getting major updates across several key trends:
1. Photonics/Networks: $GLW, $CLS, $APH
2. Memory: $STX, $SNDK, $WDC
3. Grid/Energy: $BE, $XOM, $CVX
4. Finance: $HOOD, $SOFI
5. Magnificent 7: $META, $AMZN, $GOOGL, $MSFT, $AAPL
6. AI Software: $ZETA, $LMND

Very exciting week ahead. 

If Trump doesn’t do anything out of the box, it looks like we’re in for a really strong week for the S&P 500.

I’ll be paying especially close attention to sentiment in the statements, execution, forward guidance, and industry insights - particularly in Memory, Photonics, and Energy. 

My top focus: $SNDK, $GLW, and $BE.

Some analysts are calling for 40% revenue growth at $ZETA - really curious to see if they deliver.

On $HOOD and $SOFI, I’m not expecting extraordinary results. Robinhood in particular remains heavily tied to crypto market volatility and success which has recently been low.

As for the Mag 7, five of them report this week - crazy!

I’ll be watching cloud revenue growth closely at $AMZN, $GOOG, and $MSFT. 

For $META, I want updates on AI applications across their business segments, AI project progress, and ad spend trends. 

For $AAPL, I’m looking for order numbers on their recent product launches plus any fresh details on AI projects and integrations.

text:
Amkor $AMKR is currently playing out a similar pattern to many other photonics/optics/AI supply chain companies; the Bull Flag breakout!

After months of consolidation we’ve seen a huge breakout over the last few weeks, but where do we go from here?

$AMKR is currently up about 98% YTD and is looking stretched now on almost all timeframes.

Short term; $AMKR likely needs to consolidate for a few weeks, to cool down, allow the moving averages to catch up, for institutions to accumulate more shares and to build momentum for another move up.

Fundamentally; $AMKR is the go-to advanced packaging supplier for both $AVGO and $TSMC - if Amkor can continue scaling production, expect this to continue to feed through into revenues (and profits) and further long term growth.

If $AMKR can continue to execute, their stock will continue to climb long term.

text:
Sivers Semiconductors $SIVE. The SATCOM Wildcard Nobody's Talking About 👇

Everyone's focused on Sivers as a silicon photonics / data center play. Fair. But there's a second engine quietly building underneath and it could be massive.

👉Meet ALLSPACE

They build flat-panel, multi-orbit SATCOM terminals for defense and commercial use, maritime, mobility, fixed sites. Think Starlink-grade connectivity, but hardware-agnostic across LEO, MEO and GEO.

And Sivers is their chip supplier.

Here's where it gets interesting. Each ALLSPACE terminal uses up to 1,800 Sivers chips. At up to $10,000 per terminal, the revenue math scales fast:

- 1,000 units → $10M
- 5,000 units → $50M
- 10,000 units → $100M

Volume ramp expected: H2 2026

For context, Sivers' entire 2024 revenue was ~$30M. A few thousand ALLSPACE terminals could materially move the investment case.

This is what "small volumes, big numbers" actually means in semiconductor land. You don't need mass consumer scale. You need the right customer, the right ASP, and the right timing.

text:
From our $SIVE report last night. 

Remember, we keep our reports conservative. 

Once you layer on pro‑forma net cash from the April raise, in‑place Wireless segment earnings, and legacy CST/InP photonics, total enterprise value steps up to ~SEK 4.16B (≈SEK 13.67/share).

With a further SEK 800–1,400M of unmodeled upside from IRIS², a second LiDAR win, an EU sovereign fab, and Marvell pull‑through.

Which collectively justify the convexity‑adjusted 12‑month EV range that underpins the SEK 41–45 per‑share target.

text:
It’s majority institutions moving $AXTI at these valuations, retail shorts might turn out terribly wrong.

If AXT goes down from export controls the photonics buildout does too. (Eg. AXT -> IQE -> Macom/lite) and there are bigger problems to worry about. 

If you just split out AXT’s 10+ upstream JVs into one merchant Western company it would probably be worth more strategically than 5N. 

Also revenue numbers are misleading since these are TTM contracts, not new ones signed. All the upstream materials like 7n high purity indium has spiked immensely since last year. 

It’s all vertically integrated so there might be a NAND style price hike off limited substrates, but unlike Sumitomo, AXT does the entire supply chain minus a few like red phosphorus. 

So it’s more dangerous than you think.

I’ll only say this once.
Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang just revealed the ‘Five Layer’ AI Model.
These 5 layers include;
1. Energy - $BE $OKLO $VRT
2. Compute - $NBIS $IREN $CIFR
3. Photonics - $AAOI $LITE $SIVE
4. Memory - $MU $SNDK $STX
5. Chips - $NVDA $AMD $AVGO

All of these stocks will explode in 2026 & 2027 as AI continued to expand.
Tell me, which stock are you most bullish on?

text: Huge beat on $QCOM could be signaling the bottom of the Smartphone Market, where it makes up a third of $GFS Revenue, could explain part of the surge in Volume seen on their OBV chart, instead of just a bet on the Photonics market; $GFS OBV has once again finished above it's Dec'22 ATH level, if it continues holding above past earnings, this stock will see ATHs again soon.
embedded_text:
If $QCOM sees a bottom in smartphone, it means there is also a bottom for $SOI and $GFS's core business.

If these see the weakest part of their business rebound while the bull thesis relies on photonics, there's no limit to what I'll tell my boss in a few months. https://t.co/KGNaFypf4D

For the first time, researchers at QuiX Quantum have demonstrated below threshold error mitigation in a photonic quantum computer. They used a technique called photon distillation, where multiple imperfect photons are combined through quantum interference to produce a cleaner, more identical photon. On a 20-mode photonic processor, this method reduced photon indistinguishability errors by 2.2× and achieved a 1.2× net reduction in total system error. That matters because quantum systems usually introduce more errors than they fix. This flips that balance. It could significantly reduce the number of photon sources needed, potentially by up to 4×, making photonic quantum computers far more practical 👀 In other words, this approach improves qubit quality at the source, bringing scalable, light based quantum computing much closer to reality."}}}, "rest_id": "2049521863710405103", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1738", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2049623614115246266", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "28", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
CPO, SILICON PHOTONICS, AND OPTICAL INTERCONNECT

CPO IS MOVING FROM R&D TO EARLY PRODUCTION, BUT THE NEAR-TERM DOLLARS REMAIN SMALL RELATIVE TO THE STRATEGIC SIGNAL (READ-THROUGH 10)

Call support: Management said FormFactor is “now beginning to ramp our TRITON production test system, co-developed with Advantest and Tokyo Electron,” and that the ramp is accelerating. Management attributed the acceleration to “growing volumes of CPO chips planned for later this year” and FormFactor’s leadership in Test Insertion 1, which ensures known-good die on the photonic integrated circuit wafer. Management also cautioned that CPO remains in “the very early innings” and that other insertions are “proving to be difficult for customers to implement in production.”

Affected companies: Advantest Corporation (6857: Japan) and Tokyo Electron Ltd. (8035: Japan) are positively affected with low near-term magnitude and higher long-duration optionality. The transmission mechanism is direct: TRITON was co-developed with these partners, and early CPO production test creates future attach opportunities for ATE, probers, and co-developed test platforms.

Broadcom Inc. (AVGO: US), Marvell Technology Inc. (MRVL: US), Coherent Corp. (COHR: US), Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE: US), and Fabrinet (FN: US) are positively affected with low near-term but potentially meaningful long-duration magnitude. The mechanism is validation of silicon photonics and CPO moving toward production use cases in AI networking. If CPO becomes necessary to address bandwidth density and power constraints, optical component suppliers, silicon photonics developers, and AI networking ASIC vendors benefit from a larger production ecosystem.

Teradyne Inc. (TER: US) is mixed to modestly positive. Management emphasized an open ecosystem and specifically said FormFactor works closely with Teradyne, but the named TRITON co-development partners were Advantest and Tokyo Electron. The mechanism is that Teradyne can still participate in future CPO insertions, but the earliest named production-test system appears more closely tied to Advantest and Tokyo Electron.

Near-term trading catalyst: positive for CPO sentiment but limited in revenue magnitude. The call supports the view that CPO is no longer purely a lab topic, but it does not indicate that CPO is yet a large 2026 revenue category for the broader supply chain.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: potentially high. The critical point is that Insertion 1 is focused on validating known-good photonic integrated circuits before expensive downstream packaging steps. If this becomes a standard production insertion, optical probing and electro-optical test can become recurring infrastructure layers in AI networking manufacturing.

DOWNSTREAM HARDWARE AND OEMS

AI SUPPLY-CHAIN TIGHTNESS IS POSITIVE FOR SEMICONDUCTOR VENDORS BUT NEGATIVE FOR HARDWARE OEM MARGIN FLEXIBILITY (READ-THROUGH 11)

Call support: Management described DRAM as supply constrained, HBM demand stepping up, networking demand strong, data-center CPU probe demand improving, CPO chip volumes planned later in 2026, and probe-card capacity constraints across FormFactor and competitors. The call also noted isolated cases where customers are willing to pay expedite fees in a capacity-constrained environment.

Affected companies: Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL: US), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Co. (HPE: US), Super Micro Computer Inc. (SMCI: US), Lenovo Group Ltd. (0992: Hong Kong), and HP Inc. (HPQ: US) are negatively affected with low-to-moderate magnitude on margin and delivery-risk grounds, while remaining positively exposed to AI server demand. The transmission mechanism is input-cost and availability pressure. If DRAM, HBM, probe cards, and advanced-packaging capacity remain tight, OEMs and server integrators face higher component costs, longer lead times, and reduced ability to optimize gross margin.

Apple Inc. (AAPL: US) is modestly negatively affected through memory availability and potential LPDDR/DRAM cost pressure. The mechanism is less directly AI-server related but still relevant if DRAM wafer starts are dynamically allocated across HBM, DDR5, DDR4, and other designs to maximize supplier margin.

Near-term trading catalyst: negative for gross-margin assumptions at hardware OEMs if memory price increases and AI component shortages persist.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: mixed. AI infrastructure demand supports server revenue growth, but the profit pool may continue migrating upstream toward memory suppliers, advanced-packaging providers, foundries, and test suppliers rather than downstream assemblers and OEMs.

CONSUMER SEMICONDUCTOR RECOVERY IS NOT THE DRIVER; AI INFRASTRUCTURE IS ABSORBING THE INCREMENTAL TEST AND CAPACITY DOLLARS (READ-THROUGH 12)

Call support: Management described Q2 Foundry & Logic growth as driven primarily by data-center CPU strength, with continued networking strength and “steady demand in PC and mobile.” The word “steady” is important because it contrasts with the stronger language used for HBM, networking, GPUs, custom ASICs, and CPO.

Affected companies: Qualcomm Inc. (QCOM: US), MediaTek Inc. (2454: Taiwan), Qorvo Inc. (QRVO: US), Skyworks Solutions Inc. (SWKS: US), and other mobile-exposed semiconductor suppliers are neutral to modestly negatively affected from a relative-growth perspective. The transmission mechanism is capital and capacity allocation. text:
#SIVE $SIVE #5G #6G #Beamforming #FWA #SATCOM #CHIPSAct #Semiconductors #Wireless #Nokia

Take a moment to read this, friends — we’re going to talk about the unknown side of Sivers.

A lot of investors — and me first — only knew Sivers for its photonics business (the lasers for AI datacenters). 

It’s exciting and has massive potential. But after digging a bit deeper, we discover a real hidden gem: the Wireless division.

 It still accounts for ~70% of current revenue, delivered very strong growth (+85% on some quarters in 2025), and provides solid cash-flow stability.

 It’s the quiet but powerful pillar that makes the entire $SIVE investment thesis much less risky.

First of all, what is a beamformer? (simple explanation) : 

Imagine you want to send a super-fast internet signal (5G, future 6G, or satellite connection) to a phone, a house, or a satellite.

A regular radio signal spreads out in all directions, like a light bulb lighting up an entire room. It works, but it’s very inefficient. You waste a lot of energy, the signal weakens quickly over distance, and there are lots of interferences with other devices around.

A beamformer (literally “beam former”) is like an ultra-precise smart projector:
Instead of spreading everywhere, it concentrates all the signal power into a very narrow directional beam pointed exactly at your phone or your house.

Result? The connection is much faster, much more stable, reaches much farther… and uses way less energy.

Sivers Wireless develops exactly these mmWave beamformers (chips + antenna arrays). 
This technology is essential for high-speed 5G/6G, Fixed Wireless Access (FWA = wireless internet instead of fiber), SATCOM, and defense applications.

Major wins to date (April 2026):

• January 2025 → $5.4M contract (60 M SEK) signed with a global Tier-1 ( The crowd is murmering Nokia ? ) 

It’s for the next-generation beamforming transceivers. Even better: the collaboration is planned across multiple generations.

• New → Firm $3M purchase order with Tachyon Networks (28 GHz FWA). Production and shipments already starting in 2026 — real revenue incoming.

• 3 design wins in 5G/FWA already secured → potential of around 1 million units over the first three years of production (starting 2026).

• India → Historic partnership with Intel + WiSig Networks + office opened in Bangalore to target the massive FWA market.

• Defense → CHIPS Act funding (2nd round ongoing), contracts with Raytheon, BAE Systems, Ericsson. Daybreak™ 7-15 GHz ICs now available (March 2026) — very competitive in power and efficiency.

• SATCOM → Cloudchaser chipset + Maverick antenna arrays launched (Feb 2026) + strong commercial interest.

• 6G → Already involved in ISAC research with Nokia.

Why is this strategic for $SIVE?

Wireless brings stability and predictable revenues while the AI photonics division ramps into mass production (2026-2027). It’s the financial cushion that lets the company breathe… and it has strong potential to become a real cash cow from 2026-2028.

And even if photonics hits some delays or doesn’t deliver as expected, the Wireless division alone is strong enough to justify a major re-rating on its own.

In short: $SIVE is not just an AI laser story.

With the recent news of the Photonics spin-off to Silicon Valley, $SIVE (Sweden) is effectively becoming a pure-play leader in Wireless/SATCOM/Defense, while still holding the majority of the US AI laser powerhouse. It’s the ultimate value unlock.

It’s also a powerful play in mmWave 5G/6G, SATCOM, defense, and emerging markets — with technology that is already generating revenue.

I hope this clarified some things and/or brought new info for the Sivers community!

This is not financial advice. I’m just digging into this company and sharing what I found.

3238}, {"from_index": 3710, "richtext_types": ["Italic"], "to_index": 3800}]}, "text": "#SIVE $SIVE #5G #6G #Beamforming #FWA #SATCOM #CHIPSAct #Semiconductors #Wireless #Nokia Take a moment to read this, friends — we’re going to talk about the unknown side of Sivers. A lot of investors — and me first — only knew Sivers for its photonics business (the lasers for AI datacenters). It’s exciting and has massive potential. But after digging a bit deeper, we discover a real hidden gem: the Wireless division. It still accounts for ~70% of current revenue, delivered very strong growth (+85% on some quarters in 2025), and provides solid cash-flow stability. It’s the quiet but powerful pillar that makes the entire $SIVE investment thesis much less risky. First of all, what is a beamformer? (simple explanation) : Imagine you want to send a super-fast internet signal (5G, future 6G, or satellite connection) to a phone, a house, or a satellite. A regular radio signal spreads out in all directions, like a light bulb lighting up an entire room. It works, but it’s very inefficient. You waste a lot of energy, the signal weakens quickly over distance, and there are lots of interferences with other devices around. A beamformer (literally “beam former”) is like an ultra-precise smart projector: Instead of spreading everywhere, it concentrates all the signal power into a very narrow directional beam pointed exactly at your phone or your house. Result? The connection is much faster, much more stable, reaches much farther… and uses way less energy. Sivers Wireless develops exactly these mmWave beamformers (chips + antenna arrays). This technology is essential for high-speed 5G/6G, Fixed Wireless Access (FWA = wireless internet instead of fiber), SATCOM, and defense applications. Major wins to date (April 2026): • January 2025 → $5.4M contract (60 M SEK) signed with a global Tier-1 ( The crowd is murmering Nokia ? ) It’s for the next-generation beamforming transceivers. Even better: the collaboration is planned across multiple generations. • New → Firm $3M purchase order with Tachyon Networks (28 GHz FWA). Production and shipments already starting in 2026 — real revenue incoming. • 3 design wins in 5G/FWA already secured → potential of around 1 million units over the first three years of production (starting 2026). • India → Historic partnership with Intel + WiSig Networks + office opened in Bangalore to target the massive FWA market. • Defense → CHIPS Act funding (2nd round ongoing), contracts with Raytheon, BAE Systems, Ericsson. Daybreak™ 7-15 GHz ICs now available (March 2026) — very competitive in power and efficiency. • SATCOM → Cloudchaser chipset + Maverick antenna arrays launched (Feb 2026) + strong commercial interest. • 6G → Already involved in ISAC research with Nokia. Why is this strategic for $SIVE? Wireless brings stability and predictable revenues while the AI photonics division ramps into mass production (2026-2027). It’s the financial cushion that lets the company breathe… and it has strong potential to become a real cash cow from 2026-2028. And even if photonics hits some delays or doesn’t deliver as expected, the Wireless division alone is strong enough to justify a major re-rating on its own. In short: $SIVE is not just an AI laser story. With the recent news of the Photonics spin-off to Silicon Valley, $SIVE (Sweden) is effectively becoming a pure-play leader in Wireless/SATCOM/Defense, while still holding the majority of the US AI laser powerhouse. It’s the ultimate value unlock. It’s also a powerful play in mmWave 5G/6G, SATCOM, defense, and emerging markets — with technology that is already generating revenue. I hope this clarified some things and/or brought new info for the Sivers community! This is not financial advice. text:
$LPK $LPKF 🔥
This company is not only successful in the laser and photonics sector, but also active in the solar and quantum computing space, where it collaborates with companies such as Volkswagen AG and Infineon Technologies AG.

The market cap of this company appears extremely undervalued, especially considering that its patented LIDE technology for glass substrates has massive game-changing potential.

In my view, the current valuation doesn’t even come close to reflecting its true potential.

Marvell acquired Polariton on April 22 — a company specializing in ultra-high-performance plasmonic modulators. This likely made POET redundant on the Optical Interposer side (Marvell now has its own advanced in-house solution). BUT we must not forget the bigger picture: Marvell still desperately needs a reliable light source (lasers + External Light Sources / ELS). Polariton only provides the modulator (the “switch” that turns the light on and off). It does NOT provide the actual light itself. This is exactly where Ayar Labs + Sivers come into play. Ayar Labs is significantly more advanced than most competitors with its mature SuperNova platform — already in advanced prototyping, with proven system-level integration and strong hyperscaler interest. Ayar’s SuperNova platform uses Sivers’ high-performance multi-wavelength laser arrays as the core light engine. Why Ayar Labs is significantly better positioned than most competitors: Its SuperNova platform is one of the most mature CPO/ELS solutions on the market. Already in advanced prototyping with strong hyperscaler interest. Recently dropped Lumentum and Macom to keep Sivers as its exclusive laser supplier — a major vote of confidence. BUT competition is here : Lumentum ($LITE) and Coherent ($COHR) → already nearly fully booked through 2027-2028. Smaller players like Casela, Scintil Photonics and Xscape → solid but limited in scale and capacity. Bottom line: The POET drama is hurting short-term sentiment and creating volatility across the entire photonic AI sector. However, the core $SIVE thesis remains fully intact. Jabil’s 1.6T LRO partnership (the big industrial manufacturing arm) is completely separate and unaffected. Ayar Labs stays very well positioned, and O-Net is also active. text:
Ever since I started covering photonics...

My comments were full of "$POET, $POET, $POET"

WHY? Me nor a single photonics trader I know ever thought anything of it.

Pre rev. Paid stock promo. Now wrecked trying to hype the stock then getting $MRVL mad.

Be careful buying a stock in a hot category just because "it hasn't ran yet". That's not an investment thesis.

Here’s the landscape: $MTSI – Builds high-performance analog chips that help move data efficiently inside networks $GLW – Makes the fiber and glass that physically carries data across the world $CIEN – Provides networking gear that powers high-speed data traffic $COHR – Develops lasers and photonics components used in AI and data center systems $FN – Manufactures optical hardware that connects and scales data centers $MRVL – Designs custom chips that act as the backbone for cloud and AI infrastructure $ALAB – Focused on connectivity chips solving data flow bottlenecks in AI clusters $AVGO – Supplies critical semis across networking, storage, and AI infrastructure $AEHR – Builds testing equipment for next-gen semiconductors and photonics devices $CRDO – Creates ultra-fast interconnect chips linking AI servers at scale $AAOI – Produces optical modules that enable high-speed data transmission $LITE – Designs optical components and lasers powering AI data center connectivity This isn’t just another AI theme. It’s the plumbing behind it. text:
On a day where data centers from $CIFR to $NBIS & photonics from $POET to $AAOI were pulling back strongly

Might make you think we're risk-off?

Except the rare earth/critical metals sector is greener than uranium

$USAR hit another +7% today erasing all of Friday gains as a sympathy play to $CRML's new acquisition

Can't believe it was trading for what the government paid for it last week.
embedded_text:
woah ... my $USAR thesis is aging really well

$USAR up ~15% on the announcement to acquire Serra Verde for $2.8B in cash & stock

though the deal comes with dilution

looks like the market supports $USAR acquiring the only large-scale producer of heavy rare earths outside Asia? https://t.co/QKOGh9m1lb

text:
For long-term investments (3-5+ years) in photonics/AI data links, focus on proven moats + execution over pure hype:

1. **MRVL**: Best balance—custom AI silicon + optical interconnects, real hyperscaler revenue.
2. **TSM**: Unmatched scale as the foundry powering it all.
3. **AVGO**: Networking dominance with optical edge.
4. **ASML**: Lithography monopoly for advanced chips.

High-upside but riskier: POET/AAOI if their tech scales. AXTI/AEHR solid but more niche. DYOR, diversify.

text:
Photonics stocks ride AI's need for faster, cooler data links. All have tailwinds, but vary in moat, execution risk, and hype vs reality.

Research priority (upside + strategic edge first):

1. POET (+113% PT): Optical interposers could transform chip comms—highest reward/risk.
2. MRVL (+76%): Custom AI silicon + optics; proven hyperscaler traction.
3. AAOI (+68%): GPU transceivers; direct AI cluster play.
4. AXTI (+71%): Indium phosphide wafers; foundational material.
5. AEHR (+65%): Burn-in testing; scales reliability at volume.

Start here, then TSM/INTC/ASML as anchors. Smaller ones like LWLG/POET need deeper DD on tech viability.

text:
The 7-Layer Photonics Stack

1.Materials & Wafers — InP, GaAs, silicon substrates. InP supply is the chokepoint.

https://t.co/X8pBSexGF9 & Epitaxy — Equipment to grow the crystal layers. MOCVD machines. Few players.

3.Lasers & Devices — VCSELs, DFB lasers, modulators. Where the photons actually get made.

4.Foundries (III-V Fabs) — The fabs that process compound semiconductors. Scarce and specialized.

5.Test & Inspection — Verify everything works at speed and scale. Lower binary risk, often overlooked.

6.Optics & Modules — Transceivers, MEMS, co-packaged optics (CPO). Where hyperscaler demand hits first.

7.Networking & Connectivity — Optical switches, DSPs, interconnect. The system layer.

text:
Ich habe die Liste geklaut, aber einfach um sicher zu gehen, dass auch in der deutschen Bubble ein paar Leute vom Thema #Photonic mitbekommen.

THE 7 LAYERS OF PHOTONICS

1. Materials & Wafers (substrate layer)
$AXTI, $IQE $WOLF, $COHR, $LWLG

2. Tools (fabrication layer)
$ASML, $AMAT, $LRCX 

3. Lasers (light generation layer)
$LITE, $COHR, $LASR, $SMTC 

4. Foundries (manufacturing layer)
$TSM, $TSEM, $GFS, $UMC, $INTC

5. Test, Inspection & Packaging (reliability layer)
$AEHR, $VIAV, $ONTO, $AMKR, $FN

6. Optics (module layer)
$AAOI, $POET, $GLW

7. Networking (connectivity layer)
$CRDO, $MRVL, $AVGO, $ANET, $CIEN

text:
c:7

$M7U (Nynomic AG) is a German micro-cap, off the beaten path, directly part of the photonics supply chain.

Here’s my simplified explanation of what this company does and why I like it:

AI datacenters need massive amounts of fiber optic connections to move data between chips at the speed of light. Those fiber optic components are manufactured inside specialized reactors made by a company called AIXTRON – which controls roughly 90% of the global market for these machines.

Nynomic owns LayTec – a company that makes the sensors that sit inside every single one of those AIXA reactors and measure what’s happening in real time during production. Without them, manufacturing yields drop dramatically. Near 1:1 attach rate on every reactor AIXA ships.

AIXA’s optics segment is their primary growth engine for 2026, guided at €520M revenue. LayTec rides that wave on every single order.

Now the valuation gap compared to its nearest competitors. $ONTO trades at ~12.7x sales. $CAMT trades at ~15x sales. Nynomic trades at ~1.5x sales. 

The market is currently treating it like a struggling industrial company because of the 2025 restructuring noise – but management guides for significant recovery in 2026 with disproportionately large profit expansion. Medium-term target is €200M revenue at 16-19% margins.

Given the current market cap, hitting those targets would likely force a massive re-rating toward the 3x–5x sales range typical for European specialized semi-cap players.

You’re essentially buying a hidden monopoly inside the hottest semiconductor supply chain in the world at a steep discount – before it has had a chance to run.

This is a position I opened on $IBKR last week. Not available on $HOOD. Looking to add more this week if the price dips or stays flat a little longer.

text:
$LWLG Catalyst-Driven Thesis

Catalyst-driven setup accelerating in AI silicon photonics. Pre-revenue, but de-risking fast with real foundry + IP momentum. Optical interconnect bottleneck in data centers is real — low-power, high-bandwidth EO polymers targeting 200G/400G+ lanes.

Major Recent Catalysts :

• April 29, 2026: Engaged Michael Best & Friedrich LLP (Am Law 200) as strategic IP advisor. Focus: invention harvesting, patent fortress, international filings, portfolio management & licensing/tech transfer to foundries. Clear commercialization push.

• March 11, 2026: Development Agreement with Tower Semiconductor — integrating Perkinamine® polymers into PH18 SiPho platform. Multiple engineering tapeouts planned for 2026 targeting high-speed, low-power modulators.

• March 16, 2026: High-speed modulator platform now live in GDSFactory PDK for GlobalFoundries. Plug-and-play for designers & hyperscalers.
Fundamentals Snapshot (transparent):

•  Pre-revenue (~$237K in 2025) with typical development-stage losses.
•  Debt-free + solid cash runway into 2027+ via recent raise/ATM.
•  Active ATM (normal for this stage — funding R&D/tapeouts).
•  Insider sales mostly tax/option exercises post-run (standard, not dumping).
•  ~9-10% short interest, elevated but manageable. No new red flags or scandals.

Risks vs Reward: Execution on 2026 tapeouts/design wins critical. Commercial revenue targeted ~2027. High-volatility micro-cap story stock in a massive AI tailwind (optical transceivers market → $100B by 2030).

The rally's still early in some layers. DYOR. 🚀

text:
$SIVE $LPK $ALRIB $INFQ $LWLG 🔥
Here’s my conviction portfolio - I’ve invested the most in these five, because they sit at the epicenter of the exploding AI, photonics, quantum, and advanced manufacturing mega-trends

$SIVE – Sivers Semiconductors
Leading player in high-performance photonics lasers and mmWave wireless chips. Powering AI data centers, SATCOM, 5G/6G, and optical networks with game-changing efficiency. 

$LPK – LPKF Laser & Electronics
Precision laser systems for next-gen electronics manufacturing - PCB prototyping, laser welding, and advanced materials processing. The hidden enabler behind faster, cheaper, and more sustainable chip & component production.

$ALRIB – Riber
World leader in Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) equipment - the critical tool for growing the compound semiconductor materials used in lasers, RF chips, VCSELs, and quantum devices.

$INFQ – Infleqtion
Pure-play quantum computing building neutral-atom quantum computers, precision sensors, quantum clocks, and cold-atom tech for defense, commercial, and government customers.

$LWLG – Lightwave Logic
Pioneering electro-optic polymers (Perkinamine) that deliver ultra-high-speed, low-power photonic chips. The polymer platform that could finally solve the bandwidth bottleneck in AI data centers and silicon photonics.

Did I missed something?

The thing I learned from bitcoin is that supply/demand imbalances don't get resolved overnight.

The same thing applies to AI.

There are shortages in GPUs, CPUs, data centers, power generation, photonics, chemicals, and much more.

These imbalances won't go away anytime soon.

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2439}, {"from_index": 2465, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2499}]}, "text": "What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once. That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me. Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another for defense, another for satcom, another for photonics, and maybe yet another for next-generation telecom. It is a bit like assembling the future one puzzle piece at a time, hoping the picture eventually comes together. Sivers feels different. It feels more like finding one piece that already connects several of the most important parts of that picture. At first glance, it is easy to label it as just another semiconductor company. But the more I look at it, the more that feels like an oversimplification. Sivers sits in the overlap between mmWave, photonics, datacenter connectivity, satcom and defense. Those are not niche or isolated markets. They are major long-term themes, and the company has exposure to several of them at once. That is what makes the setup so compelling. Sivers does not need every single door to open at the same time for the story to work. It may be enough for one or two of these markets to accelerate, one or two partnerships to deepen, or one part of the business to gain meaningful commercial traction before investors begin to see the company in a very different light. That is why I think the “one stock, several themes” angle matters here. For a new investor, Sivers may be easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a single narrow bet and start thinking about it as a small platform with multiple shots on goal. Not one straight road, but a crossroads. Not one engine, but several potential drivers of value that could begin contributing at different times. Of course, that does not remove the risk. Stories with several moving parts are never simple. But they can also create exactly the kind of asymmetry that makes small-cap investing so interesting, especially when the market has not fully decided how to categorize the company yet. And that is often where the upside starts to emerge. So the appeal of Sivers, to me, is not just that it has exposure to attractive end markets. It is that you do not need to buy five different companies to get that exposure. You are looking at one company that could potentially benefit from several major technology shifts at once. That kind of setup is rare. And that is why I think Sivers deserves a much closer look."}}}, "rest_id": "2045914937076183188", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "37779", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2048853695010365707", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "823", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
The CPU bottleneck could be bigger than photonics.

Because it only gets worse as AI chips get better.

A Georgia Tech/$INTC paper makes it clear. The researchers hooked up real agentic AI workloads (research assistants, coding agents, web-search agents) and stopped to watch every step.

On a cheaper GPU setup, the CPU caused 38% of the total wait time. Then they ran the same workloads on an H200. The CPU's share jumped to 65%.

A faster GPU just means you finish the GPU computations sooner, which makes the CPU wait for all the agentic actions look even bigger by comparison.

Every accelerator upgrade only sends buyers back to order more CPUs...

This is exactly why $INTC CFO said unfulfilled demand for CPUs starts with a B.

if healthcare doesn’t change in a hospital 🏥 first… …but on your wrist? $SIVE / $SIVEF 🍎🩺 That’s why I keep coming back to $AAPL and Sivers. This is bigger than “just another sensor.” This is about turning the Apple Watch into something far more powerful than a smartwatch, a health guardian people wear every single day. Blood pressure is one step. But the real prize is glucose monitoring. A future where millions of people no longer have to rely on needles, strips, and constant friction to stay on top of their health. A future where wearables help monitor the body quietly, continuously, in the background. And the opportunity goes beyond that. Not just blood pressure. Not just glucose.  Potentially hydration, recovery, respiratory-related signals, temperature-linked changes, and even sensing different things in the air around us. That’s when the story changes. The watch stops being a gadget. It becomes a guardian. And the need is massive: Diabetes already affects roughly 1 in 9 adults globally, with more than 40% undiagnosed. By 2050, that number is expected to reach 853 million. Now connect that with what Sivers has already disclosed:  follow-on design optimization work and ~50,000 laser units for its first established U.S. Fortune 100 customer, after an earlier 30,000-unit order. That’s how something world-changing often starts. Quietly.  With testing, iteration, and validation. My own view: I think this launches in 2028. And if that’s right, pre-series orders 12–18 months before launch would make a lot of sense. Now imagine Apple mass-producing health-focused consumer hardware at 50M+ units per year. That’s not just massive for Sivers.  That’s massive for the future of healthcare. From watches… to guardians. #Sivers #AppleWatch #DigitalHealth #Wearables #HealthTech #Apple"}}}, "rest_id": "2047609266886823982", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "11532", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2048849086502068634", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "755", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2439}, {"from_index": 2465, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2499}]}, "text": "What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once. That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me. Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another for defense, another for satcom, another for photonics, and maybe yet another for next-generation telecom. It is a bit like assembling the future one puzzle piece at a time, hoping the picture eventually comes together. Sivers feels different. It feels more like finding one piece that already connects several of the most important parts of that picture. At first glance, it is easy to label it as just another semiconductor company. But the more I look at it, the more that feels like an oversimplification. Sivers sits in the overlap between mmWave, photonics, datacenter connectivity, satcom and defense. Those are not niche or isolated markets. They are major long-term themes, and the company has exposure to several of them at once. That is what makes the setup so compelling. Sivers does not need every single door to open at the same time for the story to work. It may be enough for one or two of these markets to accelerate, one or two partnerships to deepen, or one part of the business to gain meaningful commercial traction before investors begin to see the company in a very different light. That is why I think the “one stock, several themes” angle matters here. For a new investor, Sivers may be easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a single narrow bet and start thinking about it as a small platform with multiple shots on goal. Not one straight road, but a crossroads. Not one engine, but several potential drivers of value that could begin contributing at different times. Of course, that does not remove the risk. Stories with several moving parts are never simple. But they can also create exactly the kind of asymmetry that makes small-cap investing so interesting, especially when the market has not fully decided how to categorize the company yet. And that is often where the upside starts to emerge. So the appeal of Sivers, to me, is not just that it has exposure to attractive end markets. It is that you do not need to buy five different companies to get that exposure. You are looking at one company that could potentially benefit from several major technology shifts at once. That kind of setup is rare. And that is why I think Sivers deserves a much closer look."}}}, "rest_id": "2045914937076183188", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "37779", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2048514663634108784", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "211", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Bottlenecks are the theme of 2026.

We’ve already had:

→ memory bottlenecks
→ photonics bottlenecks
→ CPU bottlenecks
→ GPU bottlenecks

But the next question is bigger:
What powers it all?

AI data centres don’t just need chips.
They need electricity at insane scale.

My power watchlist:

→ $CEG for nuclear
→ $VST for power generation

Don’t miss this one. A pullback soon back could happen. 

This looks a lot like buying $COHR before photonics became a core AI data center bottleneck theme.

AI photonics new age is here. If this sticks, this one could be huge. 

Revenue growth Q: +594%
Gross profit growth Q: +619%
~$69M current assets
~$66M net cash position
~8450 PS ratio 

(reflects extremely early revenue base, not mature scaling)

Still in commercialization / validation phase for EO polymer photonics

This is like buying $COHR at 15$ in 2014

All at a 2B market cap. 

We saw it with $LITE and $COHR — optical infrastructure compounds hard once AI data center demand shifts from narrative to structural bottleneck.

That’s the same structural lane where photonics becomes the invisible layer of compute scaling.

Not saying $LWLG becomes $COHR.

This is where those asymmetries live.

text:
First time I see someone on my feed recommending $AIP. It's nice to see different stocks than the traditional photonics / 800G plays that everybody hypes every day.

I'm also quite bullish on $AIP, although I sold my shares for a quick profit. What I really like is that they are actively decelerating their China business. It accounted for 25-30% before, which was a massive geopolitical headwind.

The downside for me is the royalty timeline. Those revenues only materialize a couple of years after the licensing deal is signed. So while I really like the stock, I'm not sure if it is the best timing to hold right now, but we'll see. The market reflects future potential, not current value so very possible it could go higher in the near term propulsed by INTC and AMD

And no, it’s definitely not Mycronic. This will probably be my last photonics-related Write-up for the time being.

Todas estas compañías forman parte del superciclo de photonics: la infraestructura óptica crítica para los data centers de IA (transceivers de alta velocidad, láseres InP, silicon photonics y testing).

Nota: $AXTI publica resultados esta noche después del cierre (EPS esperado: -0,07$; Ingresos ~26,2 Mn$).

El momentum en la cadena de suministro óptica de la IA sigue intacto.

¿Qué opinas? ¿Continuará el rally o es solo rotación?

text:
$AEHR It's been interesting to watch the stock the past week, following the pullback which $POET kicked off in the silicon photonics space.

$TER reported on Tuesday. Great numbers, but the stock dropped by double digits yesterday; a drop that $AEHR didn't participate much in. Teradyne is up again today.

Is the stock ahead of itself? Yes, but it's tough to fade this rally. The acceleration is clear, and it sounds like there's a strong chance of additional orders landing in May per the CEO. How much of that is priced in? We'll find out next month.

text:
Jensen's 5-Layer AI framework is the cleanest investment thesis you'll see in 2026. Energy. Compute. Photonics. Memory. Chips. Every layer is in shortage. Every layer is repricing higher.

Layer 1: Energy. AI training campuses pulling 1+ gigawatts each. Data center power demand growing 40 percent annually. Bloom Energy, Oklo, Vertiv. Bull thesis is small modular reactors and fuel cells get the AI buildout contracts. Bear thesis is grid backlogs delay the buildout 3 years.

Layer 2: Compute. Hyperscaler GPU clusters can't keep up. CoreWeave, Nebius, IREN, Cipher. The neoclouds capture overflow demand at premium pricing. Bull case is 5x revenue. Bear case is hyperscalers self-supply and squeeze them out.

Layer 3: Photonics. Networking inside data centers is the new bottleneck. Applied Optoelectronics, Lumentum, Coherent, Astera Labs. 800G and 1.6T optical transceivers sold out through 2027. Bull case is networking captures real margin. Bear case is hyperscalers vertically integrate optics.

Layer 4: Memory. The bottleneck inside the bottleneck. Micron, SanDisk, Seagate, plus SK Hynix and Samsung internationally. DA Davidson just put a $1,000 PT on Micron. Bull case is structurally extended cycle. Bear case is China NAND oversupply.

Layer 5: Chips. NVDA, AMD, Broadcom, plus custom silicon at Google, Amazon, Meta. Bull case is NVDA's CUDA moat holds 5 more years. Bear case is custom silicon takes 30 percent share by 2028 and the stack price-compresses.

What nobody's saying: this isn't a "buy everything AI" framework.

It's a structural map of where shortages compound. Every layer has supply tight through 2027. Pick the layer with the deepest moat and the cheapest valuation.

My answer: memory at Layer 4. Energy at Layer 1.

text:
Jensen's 5-Layer AI framework is the cleanest investment thesis you'll see in 2026. Energy. Compute. Photonics. Memory. Chips. Every layer is in shortage. Every layer is repricing higher.

Layer 1: Energy. AI training campuses pulling 1+ gigawatts each. Data center power demand growing 40 percent annually. Bloom Energy, Oklo, Vertiv. Bull thesis is small modular reactors and fuel cells get the AI buildout contracts. Bear thesis is grid backlogs delay the buildout 3 years.

Layer 2: Compute. Hyperscaler GPU clusters can't keep up. CoreWeave, Nebius, IREN, Cipher. The neoclouds capture overflow demand at premium pricing. Bull case is 5x revenue. Bear case is hyperscalers self-supply and squeeze them out.

Layer 3: Photonics. Networking inside data centers is the new bottleneck. Applied Optoelectronics, Lumentum, Coherent, Astera Labs. 800G and 1.6T optical transceivers sold out through 2027. Bull case is networking captures real margin. Bear case is hyperscalers vertically integrate optics.

Layer 4: Memory. The bottleneck inside the bottleneck. Micron, SanDisk, Seagate, plus SK Hynix and Samsung internationally. DA Davidson just put a $1,000 PT on Micron. Bull case is structurally extended cycle. Bear case is China NAND oversupply.

Layer 5: Chips. NVDA, AMD, Broadcom, plus custom silicon at Google, Amazon, Meta. Bull case is NVDA's CUDA moat holds 5 more years. Bear case is custom silicon takes 30 percent share by 2028 and the stack price-compresses.

What nobody's saying: this isn't a "buy everything AI" framework.

It's a structural map of where shortages compound. Every layer has supply tight through 2027. Pick the layer with the deepest moat and the cheapest valuation.

My answer: memory at Layer 4. Energy at Layer 1.

The map is real.

Pick your shovels.

today: 
"wins a $21M Texas grant to expand semiconductor and photonics manufacturing in Sugar Land.  The project adds a 210K sq. ft. facility for AI data center transceiver production and is expected to create 500+ jobs." https://t.co/wKWAy76O36

Foxconn Is Ready to Embrace CPO, as Silicon Photonic Products Manage to Reach 3.2T Transmission Capacity 

On the 29th, Guo Haozhong, director of the Foxconn Semiconductor Research Institute, delivered a speech on silicon photonics trends at the 2026 MIC FORUM Spring "Intelligent New Order" seminar. He said that Foxconn's (2317) silicon photonics products can already achieve a transmission capacity of 3.2T, but with the continued shortage of InP capacity, silicon photonics will still face 10 challenges to overcome in the future.

This year, the slogan for AI servers has changed from "fiber to copper" to "fiber and copper in parallel". This is mainly because Broadcom chips have optimized the 200G transmission speed. However, copper wires will become increasingly difficult to use, especially high-speed copper wires which are heavy and require external sheathing. The advantage of optical communication is that a single wire can carry multiple wavelengths simultaneously without interfering with each other, up to a maximum of eight wavelengths.

Guo Haozhong stated that Foxconn's modulator can already achieve a single-channel transmission speed of 200G, using 2 or 4 different wavelength lasers and employing WDM (wavelength division multiplexing) technology to integrate on a PIC (photonic integrated circuit) chip; for a single-channel 200G, if two groups of four wavelengths are used, a transmission capacity of 3.2T can be achieved, and it even has the ability to upgrade the technology to 6.4T in the future.
Since future data center transmission speeds will reach over 400G, he stated that the current single-channel transmission rate of silicon photonics is limited to nearly 200G and has high power requirements. InP has advantages in high-speed transmission, not only integrating lasers but also helping to support transmission of systems above 400G. However, InP material is not as stable as silicon, resulting in low yield and high cost. Currently, due to insufficient production capacity, many companies are also investing in the research and development of other materials, such as TFLN or SOH, which have become materials of interest.

{"from_index": 2228, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2257}, {"from_index": 2423, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2467}]}, "text": "I think one of the most underestimated parts of the Sivers / $SIVE story is the SATCOM opportunity with https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp. This is not just another customer relationship. Sivers has been working with https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp for years through several development programs worth many millions of dollars, and the relationship appears to go much deeper than a normal supplier agreement. Sivers is part of the technology roadmap for advanced satellite terminals that are built for a world where connectivity will move across different orbits, networks and operators. That is where the potential becomes interesting. According to $SIVE own material, a SATCOM terminal can contain up to around 1,800 Sivers chips, with potential Sivers value of up to around $10,000 per terminal. That changes the way you should think about scale. This is not a consumer hardware story where millions of units are needed before revenue matters. In this case, even relatively small deployment numbers can become meaningful very quickly. If https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp sells 1,000 terminals, that is up to around $10 million for Sivers. At 5,000 terminals, up to around $50 million. At 10,000 terminals, up to around $100 million. And that is from one customer platform. This is why I think the market may be underestimating SATCOM. The focus is often on when Wireless can become meaningful again, but the answer may not only come from huge 5G volumes. It could also come from a smaller number of high-value satellite terminals where Sivers has very high value per terminal. The timing also matters. $SIVE has indicated that volume ramp is expected in H2 2026. If that happens, SATCOM moves from development revenue into something much more important: recurring product revenue tied to actual terminal deployments. And the backdrop is strong. https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp is already working with demanding customers and partners such as the US Army, NATO-related defence programs, Viasat, SES and Intelsat, while defence demand for resilient satellite communication is increasing and commercial networks are moving toward more advanced multi-orbit connectivity. Terminals need to become smarter, more capable and more complex. That is exactly where Sivers beamforming technology becomes valuable. For me, this is one of the most interesting parts of the $SIVE story into 2026. Not because it requires massive volumes. But because in SATCOM, small volumes can already become big numbers. #SATCOM #Beamforming #SatelliteCommunications #SpaceTech #DefenceTech #MultiOrbit #SIVEF"}}}, "rest_id": "2049351404595163589", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "16212", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2049540957603831884", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "628", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Laser Photonics $LASE, yaklaşık 5.7 milyon adet varantın $0.70 fiyatla kullanılmasıyla ~4 milyon dolar nakit elde etti. 

Bunun karşılığında yatırımcılara, $0.975 kullanım fiyatlı yeni varantlar (Series A-5 ve A-6) verildi.

 Bu yeni varantlar hissedar onayı sonrası devreye girecek ve 2–5 yıl vadeli olacak. 

İşlem kısa vadede nakit sağlarken, ileride hisse sulandırma riski yaratıyor.

text:
Arrogance is costly.

Some of the highly regarded Korean photonics crew are about to get punished… because they’re still looking at charts on a 15-minute / yearly basis.

https://t.co/IIoZNOe7gU

“Price is king” is a lesson they still need to learn.
Warned this guy earlier (tweet below).
But we all learn at our own pace.

The real lesson: solid analyst teams and influencers with great picks, but limited experience pumping overbought stocks to their followers right before a 40–50% correction.

$IQE is headed to the 23–28 range.
We’ll be buyers then.

text:
We will be taking a short position in Semis here.
Short trade expected to last a couple of weeks. After that, we’ll look to pick up bottoms in select photonics names following a solid correction/profit-taking phase.

Trade setup:
- Using SMH, SPXL, and SOXS
- Earnings pop into mega tech earnings today
- Ideal entry on SOXS in the $13.60–$14.25 range
- Targeting 25–30% return over the next 2 weeks

Some photonic stocks will likely sell off hard (some already started) - which should create a good buying opportunity for those who missed the big rally.

Stay tuned.

This my investment thesis that I haven't publicly stated yet. And I hope people spend the time to read: From $ALRIB (quantum/MBE), $LPK (glass substrate), or $SIVE (DFB Lasers). Or even Asian names like Nippon Chemical. It's to prevent hostile actors from taking over or disrupting critical chokepoints required by America. FiconTEC (Europe) as one example (though private) was acquired by Chinese CCP affiliated companies. Not even sure how this was legal and Germany should 100% seize it back from China. They were a quasi-monopoly over testing for CPO/SiPH needed for AI and LIDAR. It's clients include the most critical players in AI and semiconductors, such as $NVDA, $TSM, $AVGO, and $INTC. Over time, significant intellectual property and technological IP transfer to China is highly likely after these acquisitions and they'll have more control over US supply chains. With enough American ownership enough spotlight on these companies: we would be in a worse shape with hidden CCP ownership/takeovers. Or we would have more backdoors on American supply chains like with $AXTI and InP substrates (if we weren't building up independent capacity now). The EU has allowed upstream supply chain chokepoints (like specialized testing or substrate manufacturing) to be bought out or have their IP transferred by geopolitical rivals. While America still not might realize a lot of these vulnerabilities. This is the most I can do as a retail investor to prevent this from happening.
